fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need to build out the coverage tracking system we've been talking about. Basically the idea is that we can record which lines of code actually ran during a real session, save that somewhere, and then pull it back later. We want it to work with at least two different storage options (like the one we used in the caching layer before, plus a simple file-based one). The tricky part is that if we run the same app on multiple servers or across deploys, the file paths are going to be totally different — we need some way to reconcile those so the reports make sense. Also we need merging: if the same file ran twice across two sessions, we don't want to overwrite the old data, we want to add it up. There should be a filtering step too so we can exclude vendor stuff and generated files. Oh and similar to the exclusion logic we wired up in that other collector module, there are a couple of built-in files that should always be excluded automatically on top of whatever the user specifies. The whole thing should be cleanly split into separate files/modules, not one big blob. We'll need a test runner that goes through a folder of JSON test cases and checks outputs. Let me know if anything is unclear but I think the shape of it is pretty obvious from context.

trigger_keywords: what are the two storage backends
which storage implementations are required
what kind of stores should be supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Two backends are required: a file-backed store (keyword: 'file') and a networked key-value store backed by Redis (keyword: 'redis'). Both must be perfectly substitutable — the high-level accumulation and reporting logic must depend only on a shared storage abstraction, not on either concrete backend.

trigger_keywords: how should null values be handled during merge
what happens to no-data positions when arrays are summed
how do we treat unexecuted lines when combining coverage
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A position marked null (no-data) means the line is not executable or was never instrumented. When merging two arrays: if both sides are null, the result is null; if one side is null and the other has a count, the result is that count (the null side is treated as zero only when the other side has an actual integer count, not null). This applies both during accumulation across snapshots and when combining arrays from two hosts.

trigger_keywords: how does path remapping work
how do we normalize file paths from different machines
what is the canonical path logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Path normalization takes an ordered list of root-path prefixes ('roots'). The last entry in the list is treated as the local/canonical root. For each prefix (in order), if the file key starts with a match for that prefix (treated as a regular expression anchored at the start), the matching prefix portion is stripped and replaced with the canonical root. The result is the canonical path. A key already under the canonical root is returned effectively unchanged.

trigger_keywords: what are the built-in exclusions
which files are always excluded automatically
what are the two hardcoded ignore rules
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Two built-in exclusion rules are always appended after the user-supplied ignore list: 'internal:prelude' (covers the language runtime's internal prelude pseudo-file) and 'schema.rb' (covers the schema definition file). The effective rule list is: user rules first, then these two built-ins in that order.

trigger_keywords: how should ignore rules be applied
how does the regex filtering work for file extensions
what is the difference between extension rules and substring rules
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each ignore rule is treated as a regular expression applied against the file key. An anchored extension rule (e.g. '.erb$') only excludes files whose key actually ends with that extension — a file path that merely contains those characters mid-path (e.g. 'a_path/that_has_erb_in/thepath.rb') is retained. A plain substring rule (e.g. 'vendor') excludes any key that contains that substring anywhere.

trigger_keywords: how should the test harness work
what does the test runner do
how are test outputs stored
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test script is at 'rcb_tests/test.sh' and is invoked as 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh'. It accepts an optional '--cases-dir <subdir>' argument (default: 'public_test_cases'). For each JSON case file and each case within it, it captures only the raw stdout from the program under test and writes it to 'rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt', then compares that file's contents directly against the 'expected_output' field.

trigger_keywords: how should the codebase be structured
what files and modules should exist
how do we split responsibilities across files
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The project must be split into at least these concern areas in separate files/modules: (1) storage abstraction + interchangeable backends (file-backed and Redis), (2) accumulation/merge core logic, (3) report normalization and filtering (path remapping, array merging, ignore-rule application), and (4) an execution adapter (the stdin/stdout JSON entry point). The adapter is the only layer that parses JSON and formats output; core logic must not depend on stdin/stdout or JSON.

trigger_keywords: how do we merge arrays of different lengths
what happens when two line arrays are different sizes
what is the length of the combined array
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When combining two line-count arrays of different lengths, the result array is as long as the longer of the two inputs. Positions present in the longer array but absent in the shorter are treated as if the shorter side has no entry; if the longer side has an integer count at that position the result carries that count, and if the longer side has null the result is null.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'store_and_read', 'read_file_lines', 'list_files', 'reset_store', 'accumulate', 'remap_path', 'combine_counts', 'build_report', 'default_exclusions'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the one we used in the caching layer before
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Redis-backed networked key-value store, which is one of the two required pluggable storage backends alongside the file-backed store.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to the exclusion logic we wired up in that other collector module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The built-in default exclusion rules appended to the user ignore list: 'internal:prelude' and 'schema.rb', as defined in the coverage collector initialization (Feature 4 / feature4_default_filter_rules.json).

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect — please check the existing codebase structure and the JSON test cases under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the precise expected behavior. The test cases are the authoritative black-box contract.

feature1_1_store_save_retrieve.json.description: Persisting a single coverage snapshot (a map of file path to per-line execution counts) through a pluggable store and then reading the full coverage map back returns exactly the same per-file line-hit arrays that were written. The same contract holds regardless of which storage backend is used.

feature1_2_covered_lines.json.description: Querying the recorded line-hit array for one specific file returns that file's per-line execution counts, while querying a file the store has never recorded returns an empty array. The contract is identical across storage backends.

feature1_3_covered_files.json.description: Listing the files tracked by the store returns the set of file keys present in the stored coverage map.

feature1_4_clear.json.description: Clearing the store removes all stored coverage data, so a subsequent read of the full coverage map returns an empty result. The contract is identical across storage backends.

feature2_1_incremental_merge.json.description: Persisting the same coverage snapshot twice accumulates line-hit counts by summing them element-wise for each file, so repeated reporting of identical execution data increases the recorded hit totals while preserving array positions that have no data.

feature2_2_merge_file_sets.json.description: Merging two coverage snapshots that share some files and differ in others sums the line hits for shared files (whose underlying source content is unchanged), retains files present only in the earlier snapshot, and adds files present only in the later snapshot. The resulting coverage map is the union of both file sets with shared files accumulated.

feature3_1_path_normalization.json.description: Normalizing a stored file key against an ordered list of root-path prefixes rewrites deploy-specific or remote roots to the configured local root, so coverage recorded on different machines or release directories maps to a single canonical path. Keys already under the local root are left effectively unchanged. Root prefixes may be plain strings or regular expressions that match rotating release directories.

feature3_2_merge_line_arrays.json.description: Combining two line-hit arrays (for instance coverage for the same file gathered from two hosts) produces an array as long as the longer input, summing counts position-by-position. A position that is uncovered on one side but executed on the other is treated as zero on the missing side; a position that is unexecuted on both sides is preserved as no-data.

feature3_3_ignore_filter.json.description: Generating a report applies the configured ignore rules to drop matching file keys from the coverage map. Each rule is treated as a regular expression: an anchored extension pattern only excludes files whose key actually ends with that extension (a file that merely contains those letters mid-path is kept), while a plain substring rule excludes any key that contains it. Files that match no rule are retained with their line-hit arrays.

feature4_default_filter_rules.json.description: Initializing the coverage collector derives its effective set of file-exclusion rules by appending two built-in internal exclusions to the user-configured ignore list, preserving order. This ensures framework-internal and schema files are always skipped on top of any user rules.
